template_description: Social-ecological system template for climate change impacts on marine ecosystems, covering warming, acidification, sea level rise, species distribution shifts, and adaptation responses.

connections.description: Multi-year ice is declining rapidly, replaced by thin first-year ice. The negative feedback reflects a trade-off or antagonistic interaction where increases in the source variable suppress or reduce the target through competitive exclusion, resource limitation, or regulatory mechanisms
Ice loss reduces hunting platform for polar bears, leading to poor body condition

Ringed seals depend on stable ice for pupping lairs; early breakup kills pups. The negative feedback reflects a trade-off or antagonistic interaction where increases in the source variable suppress or reduce the target through competitive exclusion, resource limitation, or regulatory mechanisms
Seal populations directly determine the availability of traditional marine foods
White ice surface reflects solar radiation; ice loss triggers warming amplification
Traditional marine foods are nutritionally and culturally irreplaceable for Inuit communities

Hunting practices transmit traditional ecological knowledge across generations. The positive feedback operates through direct resource dependency, where changes in the source variable proportionally affect the target variable through established ecological and socioeconomic pathways

Polar Code mandates double hulls and oil spill preparedness for Arctic vessels. The negative feedback reflects a trade-off or antagonistic interaction where increases in the source variable suppress or reduce the target through competitive exclusion, resource limitation, or regulatory mechanisms
Co-management ensures sustainable harvest levels and protects indigenous hunting rights

Moratoriums directly halt oil exploration activities in protected areas. Well-enforced marine protected areas increase fish biomass by an average of 670%, density by 446%, and species richness by 21% relative to unprotected areas

Ice algae form the base of the Arctic marine food web supporting all higher trophic levels. Sea ice loss extends the phytoplankton growing season and increases light availability, but reduces ice-algal production which forms the base of sympagic food webs
Food sovereignty needs directly drive indigenous subsistence marine mammal hunting activities
Declining ice extent directly causes earlier spring breakup, disrupting the timing of seal and seabird breeding on ice

Multi-year ice provides stable substrate for ice algal communities driving under-ice primary production. Sea ice loss extends the phytoplankton growing season and increases light availability, but reduces ice-algal production which forms the base of sympagic food webs
Food security is a core component of food sovereignty for indigenous communities. This is well-documented in Arctic Human Development Reports and IPCC AR6 CCP6. Confidence and strength upgraded.
Polar Code safety and environmental requirements increase compliance costs and restrict Arctic commercial shipping operations
The driver 'Climate change and ocean warming' is mediated through global greenhouse gas emissions that alter the climate system

Arctic warming at 2-4x the global average is the primary driver of sea ice loss. The positive feedback operates through direct resource dependency, where changes in the source variable proportionally affect the target variable through established ecological and socioeconomic pathways
Connection added to complete DAPSI(W)R(M) chain for Arctic shipping route economic viability
Connection added to complete DAPSI(W)R(M) chain for Hydrocarbon and mineral resource exploration
Connection added to complete DAPSI(W)R(M) chain for Scientific research and monitoring demand
Connection added to complete DAPSI(W)R(M) chain for Climate change adaptation needs
Arctic cruise tourism contributes negligible GHG emissions relative to global totals driving sea ice loss. The direct causal contribution is extremely weak.
Ice-edge fisheries do not causally drive sea ice loss. Fishing vessel emissions are negligible relative to global GHG forcing. Causal link is spurious.
Scientific monitoring does not causally accelerate sea ice loss. Research vessels contribute negligible emissions. Connection is spurious.

Increased underwater noise from shipping disturbs marine mammals that polar bears prey on (seals), indirectly reducing prey availability and increasing bear stress. Effect is negative on bear condition. Polarity corrected from + to -.

Black carbon deposition accelerates sea ice melt, reducing polar bear hunting habitat and prey access. AMAP (2021) documents this as a significant contributor to Arctic warming. Effect is clearly negative on bear populations. Polarity corrected from + to -.
Connection added to complete DAPSI(W)R(M) chain for Narwhal and bowhead whale habitat availability
Connection added to complete DAPSI(W)R(M) chain for Global climate stability benefit

Connection added to complete DAPSI(W)R(M) chain for Arctic Council conservation measures
Connection added to complete DAPSI(W)R(M) chain for Black carbon emission reduction agreements

The relationship between Arctic shipping expansion and indigenous food sovereignty is contested. Increased shipping may bring economic benefits but also disrupts marine mammal migration routes and traditional subsistence patterns. Evidence is mixed.
Seismic exploration does not directly cause sea ice loss. The link through eventual fossil fuel extraction and GHG emissions is very indirect at the exploration stage.
